title: How to tolerate happiness without waiting for the other shoe to drop
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
It's not a trap. But your body thinks it is. 

 You finally get something good. A moment of peace. A relationship that feels safe. A job that doesn't make...
htmlContent:
It's not a trap. But your body thinks it is.

You finally get something good. A moment of peace. A relationship that feels safe. A job that doesn't make you want to disappear. And instead of feeling relief, you feel dread. Your chest tightens. Your mind scans the horizon for disaster. You can't just sit in the good thing. You're waiting for the other shoe to drop.

I know this feeling intimately. I've had it in my own life, and I see it in almost every trauma survivor I talk to. It's not a personal failing. It's not that you don't deserve happiness. It's your nervous system doing its job. The job it learned to do to keep you alive.

Here's the thing. When you grow up in chaos or survive repeated trauma, your brain and body learn a specific pattern. They learn that safety is temporary. They learn that calm is the prelude to catastrophe. They learn that letting your guard down is dangerous. Because every time you let yourself feel okay as a kid, something happened. A parent exploded. A caregiver disappeared. A trusted person hurt you. Your nervous system built a prediction model based on that data. And now, every time you start to feel good, that model sounds the alarm.

This is what Stephen Porges calls neuroception. It's your nervous system's ability to detect safety and danger without your conscious input. And for trauma survivors, that detection system is calibrated to look for threat, even when threat isn't there. You don't decide to wait for the other shoe to drop. Your body decides for you. And then your mind scrambles to make sense of the anxiety, the tension, the feeling that something is wrong even when everything is fine.

So how do you start to tolerate happiness? How do you let yourself feel good without the impending sense of doom?

The first step is naming what's happening

I spent years thinking I was just broken. That I couldn't handle good things because something was wrong with me. I would get a compliment and feel a flash of warmth, then immediately a wave of discomfort. I would have a quiet weekend with no obligations and feel restless, almost panicked. I would experience genuine joy and then wait, holding my breath, for the moment it would be taken away.

It wasn't until I learned about the work of Deb Dana and her research on the polyvagal theory that I understood what was actually happening. She talks about the concept of "glimmers" - small moments of safety and connection that signal to your nervous system that you're okay. But for trauma survivors, glimmers can feel like threats. Because your system doesn't trust safety. It trusts survival.

Think about it. Your nervous system has a job. Its job is to keep you alive. And it's really good at that job. It learned, through years of experience, that the best way to keep you alive was to stay hypervigilant, to scan for danger, to never fully relax. Because the times you relaxed, the times you felt safe, something bad happened. So your system says, "I'm not going to let that happen again. I'm going to keep you on edge, because on edge is safe."

This is not your fault. This is not a character defect. This is a survival strategy that has outlived its usefulness. And you can change it. But it takes practice. It takes patience. And it takes a willingness to feel uncomfortable in a new way.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey. It shifts the question from "what's wrong with you" to "what happened to you." That shift matters. It changes how you see yourself.

You have to teach your body that good things can last

This is the hard part. The cognitive part is relatively easy. You can understand intellectually that you're safe, that the good thing is real, that the other shoe isn't going to drop. But your body doesn't care about intellectual understanding. Your body cares about felt experience. And your body has a long history of felt experiences that say otherwise.

So you have to build new experiences. Small ones. Incremental ones. You have to let yourself feel a moment of happiness and stay with it for five seconds instead of immediately scanning for danger. You have to notice when your body starts to tense up and say, "I know you're trying to protect me. But we're okay right now. Let's just try staying with this for a moment."

This is not about forcing positivity. It's not about toxic gratitude or pretending everything is fine when it's not. It's about building tolerance. It's about expanding your window of tolerance for positive experience. Just like you might build tolerance for difficult emotions, you can build tolerance for good ones.

I started with tiny things. A sip of coffee that tasted perfect. A moment of sunshine on my face. A text from a friend that made me smile. And when I noticed the good feeling, I would try to stay with it for just a few seconds before my mind started its usual pattern of worry. Sometimes I could do it. Sometimes I couldn't. But I kept practicing.

Peter Levine talks about pendulation - the process of moving between states of activation and settling. You can apply this to positive experience too. Let yourself feel the good feeling for a moment. Then let yourself settle. Then feel it again. You're teaching your nervous system that it's safe to feel good, and that you can come back to a regulated state after feeling good. You're not going to get stuck in happiness. You're not going to lose yourself in joy. You're just going to visit, and then come home.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay Gibson. It helps explain why your nervous system learned to expect the worst from people who were supposed to be safe.

The other shoe is a ghost from the past

Here's what I've come to understand. The other shoe isn't real. It's not an actual shoe that's going to drop. It's a memory. It's a prediction based on old data. Your nervous system is running a simulation based on what happened before, and it's treating that simulation as reality.

But the past is not the present. The people who hurt you are not the people in your life now. The circumstances that created chaos are not the circumstances you're in now. Your body doesn't know that yet. Your body is still living in the past. And it needs you to show it, gently and repeatedly, that the present is different.

This is where the work of Janina Fisher is so helpful. She talks about how trauma survivors often live in a state of "trauma time" - where the past feels present, where old threats feel current. Part of healing is learning to orient to the present. To notice what's actually happening right now, not what your nervous system predicts is about to happen.

I use a simple practice when I feel the dread rising. I look around the room and name three things I can see. Three things I can hear. Three things I can feel in my body that are neutral or pleasant. The weight of my feet on the floor. The texture of my shirt. The sound of the refrigerator humming. It sounds ridiculous. It feels ridiculous. But it works. It brings me back to the present moment, where the other shoe is not dropping. Where I am safe. Where the good thing is still happening.

You can do this too. When you feel the happiness and then the dread, pause. Breathe. Look around. Notice what's actually happening. The good thing is still there. The other shoe has not dropped. And maybe, just maybe, it's not going to.

Practice staying in the good

I'm not going to lie to you and say this gets easy. It doesn't. But it gets easier. Your nervous system can learn new patterns. It can update its predictions. It can start to trust that good things can last, at least for a little while.

The key is practice. Deliberate, repeated, imperfect practice. You will fail at this. You will feel good and then immediately spiral into worry. You will have moments of peace and then your mind will find something to catastrophize about. That's okay. That's not failure. That's the process.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) by Arielle Schwartz. It gives you concrete exercises for working with these patterns.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did you feel a little bit of hope? A little bit of skepticism? A little bit of both? That's normal. That's your system trying to figure out if this is safe. Stay with that feeling. Don't push it away. Just notice it.

I want you to try something. Think of one good thing that's happening in your life right now. It can be small. It can be as small as a good cup of coffee. Let yourself feel the good feeling for five seconds. Don't try to make it bigger. Don't try to hold onto it. Just feel it. And then notice what happens. Does your mind start to find problems? Does your body tense up? Does the dread arrive?

If it does, that's information. That's your system telling you it's trying to protect you. You can thank it for that. You can say, "I know you're trying to keep me safe. But I'm okay right now. Let's just try this." And then try again. Five seconds of good feeling. That's all.

You can build from there. Ten seconds. Thirty seconds. A minute. You're not trying to feel good all the time. That's not realistic for anyone, trauma survivor or not. You're just trying to build a little more capacity to tolerate the good when it comes. To not push it away. To not wait for the disaster. To let yourself have it, even if it's temporary.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. It's full of small practices for building nervous system capacity.

The other shoe might never drop

I don't know if the other shoe is going to drop. None of us know that. Life is unpredictable. Bad things happen. Good things end. That's the reality of being human. But here's the thing. The waiting, the dread, the hypervigilance - that's not protecting you from the bad things. It's just stealing the good things. It's making you suffer twice. Once in anticipation, and once in the actual loss, if the loss even comes.

You deserve to feel good. You deserve to let yourself have the good things without the constant scanning for disaster. You deserve to sit in the happiness and let it be real, even if it's only for a moment. Even if it's only for five seconds.

Start there. Five seconds of happiness. Five seconds of letting yourself have it. And then another five seconds. And another. Build from there. Your nervous system can learn. It can change. It can start to trust that safety is possible, that good things can last, that the other shoe might not drop after all.

And if it does? You'll handle it. You've handled everything else. But right now, in this moment, the shoe is not dropping. The good thing is still here. And you can let yourself have it.

Healing Journey Resources

  • What Happened to You? (paid link) by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay Gibson
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) by Arielle Schwartz
  • Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana
